paper_map.title: Temperature dependence of the deformation behavior and mechanical properties of a Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steel for cryogenic application
paper_map.abstract_summary: The temperature-dependent deformation and mechanical properties of an Fe–20Mn–6Al-0.6C-0.15Si austenitic low-density steel were investigated via tensile and Charpy impact tests at −196°C, −77°C, and room temperature. Decreasing temperature reduces stacking fault energy, promoting planar dislocation glide and increasing low-angle grain boundary fraction, which improves both tensile strength and elongation. In contrast, impact toughness at cryogenic temperature is lower because crack propagation becomes unstable earlier, although crack initiation resistance is higher; secondary cracks at room temperature enhance toughness.
paper_map.research_objective: To investigate the temperature dependence of deformed microstructures, work hardening behavior, and mechanical properties of an Fe–20Mn–6Al-0.6C-0.15Si austenitic low-density steel, providing theoretical support for its cryogenic application.
main_process_variables: melting and casting in vacuum induction furnace, air cooled to RT
tensile test at −196 °C, −77 °C and room temperature
Charpy impact test at −196 °C, −77 °C and room temperature
test temperature (as primary variable controlling SFE and deformation mechanisms)

key_sections.description: Summarizes key findings on temperature dependence of deformation and mechanical properties.
Background on austenitic low-density steels, SFE-dependent deformation, and motivation for cryogenic application.
Alloy composition, melting, forging, homogenization, and mechanical testing procedures.
Presents initial microstructure (EBSD, XRD), tensile properties and stress-strain curves, deformed microstructures at different temperatures, and Charpy impact data and fractography.
Analyzes deformation mechanisms (effect of SFE and temperature on planar glide) and reasons for poor cryogenic impact toughness.
Summarizes three main conclusions about strength-ductility synergy at low temperature, crack initiation vs propagation, and fracture toughness features.
